parentQuestion: A major function of serous membranes is to decrease friction.

scaffolds.q: Organs in body cavities move constantly (heart beats, lungs expand, intestines churn).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! All this movement could cause friction damage without lubrication.

scaffolds.q: FRICTION is the resistance when surfaces rub against each other.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Friction causes heat and wear. Not good for delicate organs.

scaffolds.q: SEROUS membranes secrete a watery, slippery fluid.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This serous fluid acts as a lubricant between the membrane layers.

scaffolds.q: The serous fluid fills the space between parietal and visceral layers.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! It creates a slippery layer that reduces friction.

scaffolds.q: Without lubrication, your heart beating would create painful friction.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Pericardial fluid lets the heart slide smoothly as it contracts.

scaffolds.q: Pleural fluid lets lungs expand smoothly against the chest wall.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! You breathe ~20,000 times per day - that's a lot of sliding!

scaffolds.q: The PRIMARY function of serous membranes: REDUCE FRICTION.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Lubrication is their main job.

scaffolds.q: Serous membranes reduce friction between organs and cavity walls.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This is their major function.

scaffolds.q: The statement is TRUE - decreasing friction is a major serous membrane function.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Serous fluid lubricates to reduce friction during organ movement.
